Iulia’s YouTube library:

![][image1]  
If you've played with a microcontroller before and made an LED blink, congratulations. You've taken your first step towards robotics. Now, how do we get from here all the way to building humanoids that are capable of autonomously performing new tasks? \[music\] It's quite a journey in between. This video will be your map and guide, taking you from zero \[music\] and slowly adding more complexity, different techniques, more integrations from real life use cases until we get to the state-of-the-art of robotics. Today, we're going to take a look at what this journey looks like. what are all the chapters you should pay attention to and learn more of so you can call yourself a physical AI engineer. That is not to say that this is an all-in-one tutorial that will get you there within a few minutes. \[music\] There's hours and hours that you are going to have to put into these various topics. But if you're wondering how to get started and structure this path, that is what we're going to give you. Throughout this journey, we're going to be taking a project and building it together, adding more complexity as we go. You can expect to have \[music\] the relevant resources and options for where you can deep dive and why. and you can expect to directly see the implementation of what we're talking about and why these concepts are helpful and how they fit within the bigger scheme. That being said, there's a few things that you should already be a little familiar with that will make this a lot easier to follow along with. We're \[music\] talking about some basic programming, ideally Python or C++, some math concepts like linear algebra or geometry which will really \[music\] help when we get to transforms and things like 3D modeling and some understanding of machine learning and data science \[music\] like how to handle data, train models and so on. Some of these I already have some videos on, so I'll already link those in the description. Then our path looks as follows. We start with microcontrollers because they're the most accessible and easy to get your hands on. We can build projects directly from scratch and try to understand how each of the components work, put them together, start to add \[music\] custom code, and go beyond just learning and following from tutorials until you create something of your own. For example, building a robot arm. There's going to be multiple iterations of this. And with each new phase that we unlock, we can make this project even cooler and more futurep proof. Next up, if you want to go from toy project to real industry standards, \[music\] we're going to have to learn ROSS. ROSS or the robots operating system is widely adopted within the industry \[music\] and pretty much a standard across all organizations that work with robotics. With the exception of some proprietary software, of course, Ross is one of the most useful tools that you can learn. Ross defines the way the robots \[music\] organize themselves from nodes and topics, subscribers. It helps us standardize not only the way that components are defined, \[music\] but how they can communicate with each other and be put together into systems. This way, you can work on multiple types of robots, but always work with the same infrastructure and pull pieces that other people have created. So, we can take the robot arm we've defined with microcontrollers and processors \[music\] and use the ROS standards on top of it to create something that is more robust \[music\] and modular and can be included in even bigger, more impressive projects. At this point, we can have a representation of our silly arm that we built at home that is created exactly the same as the most advanced robot arm out \[music\] there. And we can start to create functions and components and algorithms that would work on either of them. So, you don't need to have the most expensive software out there. You \[music\] can just have a simulated version of it. Along with the concept of digital twins, simulations \[music\] allow us to work with systems before having to build them in real life. So we can optimize, create new features, and test things out \[music\] without having to break the bank or break a real prototype. Especially when we get to the later parts of adding AI to our robots. Being able to generate a lot of data and have the robots simulate and \[music\] experiment with a lot of different scenarios and a lot of different tasks is invaluable. We'll work with things like 3D modeling to put our simple robot arm into a simulation and see what \[music\] we can train on top of it. Now that everything is set up in Ross, this will be pretty easy to integrate. We're going to take some time to understand how images or videos can be \[music\] processed and what information can be taken from there. So, not all of it looks like magic, \[music\] but rather a lot of really cool mathematical and programming techniques. We're going to be taking a look at libraries like OpenCV and how we can build some really cool solutions based on the cameras that our robots can interact with. We're also going to take a look at all other kinds of sensors that our robots collect data \[music\] from and what we can learn from that data and how we can use it to make the robots smarter. Very important to note that a lot of this is not actually AI. It's just smart use of math and programming all the way to some elements of data science and maybe machine learning. But there's a lot of really smart and optimized things that we can do here that have been developed over years that are very important to understand before we get to the part where we rely on AI. which is not to say that we're not going to explore that as well. We would be aiss not to mention and use the amazing developments that have come along with LLMs like Chat Gupt or Claude. We've seen the wide adoption of those across things like chat or agentic functions, but I think some of the most impressive applications are in the fields of robotics. We can take large models and combine them with everything we've learned so far about the use of camera and vision and generating actions based on your environment. And with things like VALAs or models like Pi Zero, we can make our robots really intelligent and able to adapt to new things themselves. So our simple, humble robot arm can be rewritten with Rust, simulated and modeled and generate \[music\] training data to deploy its own intelligent model to help it learn new tasks. We've seen this firsthand with projects like the robot, which takes a very simple 3D printed robot arm with the same kind of motors that we play \[music\] with in electronics projects, but able to deploy really advanced models on top of it and with the use of a few various cameras, \[music\] learn to understand its environment and act autonomously. So just imagine in a few videos we can go from a simple humble robot \[music\] arm to exploring 3D printing, ROSS simulation, training AI models, all the way to having our autonomous robot arm do tasks that \[music\] we train it to do completely by itself without us having to even touch it. I'm really excited to get started on this journey with all of you and take you along on this learning path. Whether you're also starting from zero or jumping in along the way, \[music\] each video will aim to unlock a new capability that you can immediately try out and put into practice yourself. This is all about getting your hands dirty and trying \[music\] to build something yourself so you really understand how it works. To follow along, make sure you subscribe. \[music\] Let me know in the comments what you're starting with and what you're most excited to see in this series. And we're going to be building this journey \[music\] together. Let's get started.

![][image2]  
Hello everyone. This is Julia and welcome back to engineering. I have built a robot arm. Well, technically I've built two robot arms and you could technically count these as well. So, I've kind of built four robot arms and today we're going to build one together. Now, I wouldn't say I have a problem. I would say I have experience. So, in today's video, I'm going to teach you everything I've learned from this process, and together we're going to build the ultimate robot arm without any of the mistakes that I've made along the way.

Let's get started. On this channel, we're on a journey of going from zero all the way to physical AI. There's a lot of steps in between, but let's first look at how most people get started. When you get into robotics, sure, you could start with AI or Ross or mechanical engineering if you're really studying it. But that's not how most people get into the field. Instead, what is most accessible and popular, and the great thing about being into robotics today, most people get started with one of these, an Arduino, a Raspberry Pi, a Raspberry Pico, an ESP32, or whatever version of a microcontroller you have access to. They're within most people's budgets. They're very easy to get in most countries and there's a lot of information and resources out there to get you building. And from there, all you need is some of these servo motors. From the very simple, cheap one that most everybody gets in one of their starter kits and to the more advanced and powerful to something like actuators or stepper motors or the fancier ones that you find in more expensive and advanced robot arms. It's all about making sure the joints can rotate. You got the board, you got the servo motors. How do we go from this to an actual arm that can move? In my previous videos, I've kind of tried it all. You can make something out of cardon and tape, plastic, whatever bits you have around the house. As long as the motors can be put together to form joints, you have a robot arm. However, what I found to be most useful and flexible and something that makes being in robotics today really, really exciting is the revolution of 3D printing. These are both 3D printed robot arms. This one has a very simple model that is built around the S90 servos. And this one is a more complex design that is a bit more robust and has more expensive hardware in it. However, they both start the same way. You have a design file and then a printer just spits them out of PLA fabric. This means that whatever you can think of, you can design it and then you can hold it in your arms. And I think that's pretty badass. However, it's not really as easy as it sounds. There's a lot that goes into this design process like CAD and there's a lot of skills to learn to be able to design these files yourself. But not to worry, one of the best parts is that there's a lot of models that are open source and share it online. So, you can find a lot of these and you don't have to design it from zero. In fact, all of the robots that I'm going to show you today are linked below and you can just open the files and play with them yourself as well. And not to worry, you don't even need to have a 3D printer yourself. If you have the files, there's a lot of services out there that can help you to get them printed and shipped to you. Which brings us to the sponsor of today's video. Just way provides CNC services and different types of printing, including metal, and in this case, 3D printing, which is what we've done for this video. It was very simple. I just uploaded their files on the website, selected the material that I wanted, the color, the infill, and a few other settings, and then they just gave me a quote, and sent it to my house. So, this is very convenient if you don't have a 3D printer yourself. Thank you, Just Way, for supporting the channel and sponsoring this video. Which leads us to another question. With so much choice out there, how do you choose? This is the first arm that I've had printed. It has a very, very simple modular design. It really just relies on the actual servo motors and their hats, which keep together these joints and give it flexibility. It served me really well in the beginning, but then I got a little curious and I thought we could do something a bit more fun. Which brings us to this. I was browsing on Thingiverse throughout the most popular robot arm designs and I found this, the Easybot MK3. This is the third iteration of previous designs and I'll link here the creator for them and again the file is in the description. But you'll notice that this differs from the first design quite a bit in terms of the physics that it uses. There's a lot of gears involved. These arms are controlled from the base rather than having rotors along at every joint. And it has more complex and interesting types of movements. It's also controlled with these stepper motors. Instead of having precise positions, they can move joints to. they continuously rotate and you can define the direction and the speed. So this is quite a different way to approach a robot arm. And now while I thought that that would be a very interesting experience to try to go with these more advanced sort of joint movements and learn a bit more about things like reverse kinematics and physics by trying to design how the way you program the stepper motors will actually define the movement of the arm. I will admit I kind of got stuck with the whole process. the design tends to get a little bit stuck along the way. Doesn't really go as smoothly as you would want it to. So, when the stepper motor that isn't also that strong tries to get the whole thing to move, it's quite slow. It was, however, interesting for trying out a new technique and trying to get the stepper motors and their little plates to connect and work with the Arduino. That was quite fun and I would still consider it a very nice skill that I got along the way. But this was, as far as I'm concerned, a bit of a failed project. And it really got me to appreciate my original baby in a whole new way. I mean, the simplicity, the ease, and how quickly I got this together and installed. One of the worst parts about this design is the amount of screws that it took. I now have endless boxes of screws in every dimension, thickness, and length. And it took so long to put all of these together. But this was just so elegant and simple. So nice in fact that I decided to just print another one. For this project, I also finally bit the bullet and got my own 3D printer as well. While ordering the files, which is what I've done with all of the robots so far, is very smooth and a great process. When you get to the point of having seven plus robot arms in your house, it may be time to start printing at home as well. So, I've used the same files that I sent to the printer for this one and designed them at home with just a different color. However, that is not to say that we're going to repeat the same arm again. No, no, no. No two robot arms are created equal because like I mentioned, there's so many dimensions that you can make changes on. Even if I've settled on the hardware that is part of the robot design, there's still the matter of controlling it. The videos I made with this robot arms are mostly aimed around Raspberry Pi Pico, which can be programmed in Micro Python. Now, I prefer this because coming from the data science world, Python is my language of choice. However, I did a poll on the channel and most of you work with Arduino and ESP32 and by extension with C++. So, I thought I would challenge myself and reproduce the robot arm with that stack as well. So, let's get this out of the way and we're going to build it together. We're starting with servo motors. For this build, I'm going to be using this ESP kit that I got sent by Eligu. Thank you guys for sending this to me to try out. link in the description as always. And my favorite part is that they also sent me this an incredibly overengineered screwdriver. So, we're going to start assembling this together and we'll keep talking about the design. As you can see, the main principle of this robot arm is that the different joints connect on top of each other. So you see the little bumps and the servo motor heads and these kind of just click on top and then when you use a little hat to secure it and click it into place. Now because this 3D print was the maiden voyage of my new printer, it's not all ending out perfect, which is fine. If you need to, you can whittle away whatever changes need to be made so that these pieces fit together. This is another reason why using a 3D printing service like the sponsor of today's video is sometimes just the easier option because they actually know what they're doing. And there we go. That was incredibly smooth and nothing went wrong whatsoever. Um, as a design choice, I've just decided to skip this one for the very first time. And I feel like this looks a little bit smoother. Um, and I've also decided that, you know, it's a little boring when all the joints move perfectly. So, that's a healthy joint. And this

is a fun joint. That's a loose, flexible, fun joint as well. And uh, that's also an exciting design choice. These are all design choices and not at all issues and mistakes. Keep a positive framework. Things we've learned. This screwdriver is actually amazing. This is one of my new favorite sounds.

Uh, what we've learned is that I need to recalibrate my 3D printer a little bit because all the extra filament around the very precise joints makes it a little difficult to assemble things. You could do a little bit of whittling along as you want to get them to fit a little bit better, but for certain issues like this mouthpiece, I think it's actually designed for a different model of rotor hat. So, this one doesn't quite fit in there perfectly. The next step, of course, is to just learn Blender or CAD myself and make a new version of this file. I will attempt to do that in a future video and I'll come back and link a new file that I designed myself back there. It's one of my goals to design my own robot arm. This is how this happens. You try things, you hit different walls, and in order to go forward, you need to study new components of this robotics field. So, I'll still call this a success. And of course, we still have the actual functioning one that I was able to assemble. All right. Next up, we're connecting all the wires according to the pin information. You can Google these for whatever model of microcontroller you have. So you can plug in the middle pin from each servo motor to one of the GPIO pins and make sure you link it to the power and ground of the microcontroller as well. So this is the original code that we've written in Python. It runs as follows which also starts hinting at another future concept that we're going to be exploring. Wouldn't it be great if we had a way to abstract whatever type of code you're writing or whatever type of hardware you have? As long as you're just defining, hey, I want a robot arm to move through these positions, and I want it to pick up a tissue, I should be able to do this quite simply without having to rewrite it every time I get a slightly different robot arm or I get a slightly different piece of hardware. And that's what we're going to look at in the next episode with Ross, the robot operating system. But before we get to that, we still have the difficult process of doing it by hand. So there we go. I've installed the ESP32. I think I want to first try it on this with the broken hardware just to kind of see what happens.

Um, okay. This doesn't happen in software engineering. Let's do a little palette cleanse. Look at it just going along. There's no weird sounds. Everything's just \[sighs\] smooth. So hopefully by the end of this video, you now have a better idea of the kind of robot arms that are possible to build, what could go wrong when you do it, and what's maybe a good choice for you to make so you avoid a lot of these issues. Starting with the design of the arm, you can make different choices if you want to work with servo motors or with stepper motors, if you want to work with gears or just the movement of the servo hat, all the way to the more advanced hardware that is more expensive but gives you more flexibility like the SO101 arm. Then when it comes to developing the actual arm, you can go with 3D printed bodies which make it very flexible to design it the way you want. If you know how to design things, that is. You can use files from the internet or you can learn to make your own CAD which we'll cover in a future video. When it comes to the actual code, you can use various type of microcontrollers. You just have to learn to code them in their particular way. You have to choose if you want to learn C++ or MicroPython and how you're going to build them up, which library you're going to leverage from online and then how to wire them appropriately as well. So you have to put in a lot of different skills from software engineering, electronics, mechanical engineering, design and simulations and much more. But I think that's what makes the robotics field so interesting. You don't need to be an expert at everything. You can kind of just make it work with what you've got. Just expect to spend some time debugging, fixing, experimenting, and learn to have fun with the mess. I certainly do. So with so with that in mind in the next episodes we'll take a look at Ross and how we can abstract away some of these minute programming issues. How we can learn to make simulations and online representations of our arms which might be a little less painful than having components jumping out everywhere. Probably won't be though. Probably be a whole new different torture. But hey, that's what we signed up for. Let's keep going towards physical AI. I'm your host, Julia, and I'll see you in the next

![][image3]  
Wow, this is advanced robotics, guys. This is peak physical AI. On this channel, we're on a journey of going all the way from zero to physical AI with a lot of hands-on experience, a lot of trial and error. Well, mostly a lot of error, but figuring it out together? Because really, when you have the right attitude, how hard can all of it really be? And today, we're really going to put that to the test. I'm giving myself one day to learn Ross. You know, notoriously one of the most complex and hard to get parts of robotics. Why am I doing this? Well, frankly, because I'm severely behind schedule. See, my first plan was to start learning Ross with hardware because who doesn't like looking at a really fancy robot doing a lot of cool stuff? That's what I've been doing on this channel so far. And I even managed to lend this amazing deal where the University of Amsterdam is going to land me one of their rovers. Well, actually, how cool would it be to go from a 3D printed DIY robot arm to this with LAR and sensors and being able to automatically follow paths and do a lot of stuff with Ross? Pretty cool, right? Well, it's not happening and I am very bitter about it. I was so so so excited to get this. I got it home from the university on buses and trains. It we went on quite the journey together until eventually it made it to my studio where I took it apart and we looked inside together. We checked out the Nvidia board that powers the whole thing because robotics, physical AI, and Nvidia are pretty much synonymous these days, and I was so excited to get started with it on my own. Instead, what happened is that I somehow managed the impossible and burned the robot. Sadly, I wasn't filming at the time, but here's a dramatic reenactment.

Now, I'm still not 100% sure what happened. This rover has a lot of parts in it besides the Nvidia board which should be more or less protected from shortcircuiting, but there's also some electronics board in there. However, I am too terrified to plug it in again and know for sure. What we should do is take apart all the parts and test them separately. I do not have the strength to go to another failed hardware project. So, instead, we're going to focus on Ross the software way today. And really, when you think about it, this just puts into perspective how incredibly ridiculous learning robotics is. Think about when you started to learn coding. If you make a few mistakes and you probably made a lot of them, you just get error messages. You just get pop-ups and you just have to fix it and it's fine. Now, imagine that every single time your code failed to compile, there's a pretty high risk that your code just starts smoking or exploding instead. How would anyone learn anything? And that's what I want to ask to the entire field of robotics because I'm feeling pretty discouraged right now. However, it doesn't matter. We must persevere and go on. So, what I'm going to do instead is try to set up Ross on my computer. Try to get some of the basic hello world equivalents, which is of course the turtle sim. figuring out how that works, getting a basic understanding of the concepts, and maybe trying it out on one of my Raspberry Pies that I have at home, just because if I burn that, it's a lot easier and cheaper to replace than a 1K rover. Yeah, that one still hurts. So, we're going to walk through it together. We're going to figure out the Ross concept, and at the end, hopefully, we have something running without any smoke. That is the new threshold that we judge all our projects by. Let's go. So, the first thing to know about Ross is that it notoriously mostly only works on Linux. So, if you have a Windows or a Mac computer, well, kind of out of luck. Although, there are some creative ways to get around it, which we'll take a look at. But mostly, the best thing for you to do is to get some kind of Linux system running. Now, in my case, I have my PC set up here, and I've dual booted my Windows PC to also run Linux in parallel. I just installed Mint on it. and we're going to try to work in this virtual environment instead. Now, how most people would learn Ross is well, probably within a classroom if you're studying some kind of robotics degree or by going through course materials. There's a lot of really good videos on YouTube as well. But from what I've seen, there's like hours and hours worth of playlists that just get you through setting it up. I really don't want to go through all of that. So, we're going to compress it as much as possible. And I'm actually going to try to use Claude for this video to see if that speeds up the process at all. So, how do I start with Ross? First thing first, figuring out which ROSS to install. Check out which Ubuntu system you're running. In my case, it's even one layer deeper. I have Linux Mint 22.2 to cinnamon, which is equivalent to the Ubuntu 24, which then means I need to use ROSS 2 Jazzy because apparently Linux people really love making it very hard for other people to understand what is it that they're talking about. It's just code names on top of code names. And I feel like we're going to run into that a lot. But regardless, I know I need Ross Jazzy. So, let's try to install that. I'm going to do pseudo. Apparently, my Ethernet cable's not in. Noble Jammy Bookworm. Are you serious? Why? Why are there so many instructions just to download it? Okay, a lot of stuff is happening. Still going. Still going. No, still going. 20 minutes later. Okay. So, I guess be prepared to spend your first hour of learning Ross installing Ross.

What does this mean? Open a new terminal. Okay.

I just want to control my little to play sim. Okay, so we installed Ross, but then it says command not found because I didn't add it to the path. So, we're going to add Ross to the path. And now when I say Ross 2, it actually works. Then can I do ROSS 2 node list?

open a new terminal and install RQT. We are on our third terminal, so you know things are getting serious. I don't know what I'm doing, but it's so much fun. Oh, agree. info.

All right, we are now severely pushing towards our eight, maybe even nine. So, we got to wrap this up and make it work now. So, what we've done is we've set up the Raspberry Pi over here. I got it running with my external screen and keyboard and mouse. And we got to testing how stuff works. We installed Ubuntu. We got the Ross running exactly like we did on the Mint computer, only this time on this tiny little computer instead. Then I actually used SSH to control it from my laptop so I have a little bit more control and because I wanted to use cloud code again because of the amount of dependency issues that I've had to run through. It's basically been about one or two hours of just trying and forcing installing of packages over and over and over. I had to downgrade packages. I had to hold packages. I had to reinstall packages and add new different versions of different packages. And in the end it still did not work. So, what I ended up doing is using Pixie, like I hinted at at the beginning of this video. It basically bypasses and pip and UV and all of those together and creates this binary that just runs on whatever platform or environment you need. So, that's how you could run Ross on a Mac as well. And in this case, even though we had Ubuntu, it was still so incredibly annoying to put together. So, we're using Pixie anyway. And we can actually use Pixie both on this Raspberry and on the Mint computer. and we can get them to communicate, which is what we're going to do because another blocker was the fact that on this Raspberry Pi, we're only using the server version. This is all we can see. So, obviously, we can't really run the turtle sim visualizer here because all of my beautiful faces knocking against the walls would simply not render in the command line. So instead, we're going to set this up as the node that controls all the microcontroller electronicy bits, namely this setup over here. And that's like the whole point of Ross. Anyway, we're able to run different nodes in different environments. Each of them doing the best of their abilities. In this case, this node will be our little output that can either turn on the light or move the servo motor. And just to make sure, I first tested these with some very basic Python skills. I just ran some code on the Raspberry Pi to flash these little light bulbs and to move the servo left or right. This is very very basic stuff, but the complex part is that the ROSS node is going to be controlling this rather than this very simple line of Python. So, we have this set up here to be the output. We finally got the pixie to run. And it now says that it's waiting for a command\_vel. That is the node that this communication is going to happen on where the actions are going to be sent. And I'm going to go back to my Windows PC that has mint on it. And I'm going to run Ross there and run the turtle sim. Tell the turtle sim. And that should control this. There you go. you can uh monitor the situation for us and then let's go. All right, so this is where our original raw setup happened. We're transferring this into Pixie as well because that should be a little bit easier. We no longer need the hexagon turtles. Instead, we're just trying to run the very basic simulation and get it to work on the same communication layer as we have on our Raspberry Pi. Okay, so we're using the Ross domain ID42.

And hopefully this should do something. I will be honestly quite surprised at this point if it works cuz it's been such a day. Guess what? A new terminal. Hopefully the last one for a while. Of course, you do not know what Pixie is. Okay, this is ridiculous. I would love to run Pixie install. Oh, that's I'm terrified that this works. Wait, wait.

I'm hearing the servo. Wait. Oh my god. Oh my god.

The light is on. Holy \[ \_\_ \]

This is so insane. So my my my oh my god my computer over here, it's controlling from the Ross node on my computer all the way to to the to the Raspberry Pi thing over there.

Holy \[ \_\_ \]

Oh my god,

this is physical real. Oh my god, I've actually done it. I'm not going to lie, I didn't believe that this would actually work. Okay, well, I think that's um a great natural stopping point for this video. It is now almost 1000 p.m. Um, did I learn Ross in 8 hours? Hell yeah, I feel like I did. Oh, this is exhilarating. This It was one thing to get like the Raspberry Pi and a microcontroller stuff to work, but to get to control it from like across the room over this network with like custom nodes, this unlocks so much more. And I'm so excited for what comes next. We're going to run simulations. We're going to do some amazing visualizations and really, really pretty things. And so much more complex systems than this. A lot of stuff coming. We're just getting started. A lot more mistakes to come for sure, but today we had a success and I'm so so happy with this. I'm going to try to publish all the code that I worked through. I'll put the details in the description. Thank you so much for watching and for following along. We're in this together. I'm so proud of us and I'll see you in the next video. The  
![][image4]Everything you see a robot do, from seeing things and processing the environment around it to planning, deciding to move, and actually moving, involve around 10 different pieces, all working together. Most robotics tutorials assume that you already know all of that. This one doesn't. In the next 20 minutes, I'll give you a working understanding of all the concepts you need in order to work in robotics. You're not going to be an expert, but you're going to be oriented and have a very clear picture of what it is you need to learn and where to start, which is the number one question I see beginners ask. Hi, this is Julia and welcome back to engineering. This is episode zero of a new series I'm doing together with seed studios and NVIDIA hardware, where we're going to go through all the different parts it takes to get into physical AI and specifically the Jetson ecosystem. Robotics is a very complex multi-disiplinary field. So to get a better grasp, here's the mental image that I want you to keep throughout this entire tutorial and series. At the bottom, we have the hardware or the computer brain. This is what everything will run on. This is our infrastructure that allows the computations to happen. On top of that, we control it with an operating system. We're then building program with code and different programming languages allowing us to implement things like AI modules and we have robot specific tools as well. So let's explore the stack from the bottom up. In terms of hardware, you're probably used to working with your laptop or your PC. If you've done electronics or beginner projects, you probably used microcontrollers or microprocessors. These are the things that power up your robots. The main computer that we're going to use in this series are CPUs and GPUs. Computer processing units and graphical processing units. Specifically, GPU is what allows us to run more advanced machine learning models like neural networks. GPUs used to be what powered gaming, but they're now used for parallel processing, multi-threading, and allowing us to do a lot of different tasks at the same time. Things like CUDA from Nvidia allow us to control how the hardware is going to be processing the task that we send to it, distributing work along different nodes and different workers, making it a seamless process that allows you to go from doing one task at a time very slowly to doing a lot of different computations that work together. This will be very useful for things like working with matrices and everything that has to do with the data science behind robotics. Now, you can have this hardware at home or you can leverage things like cloud services that send your requests to different computers that are incredibly powerful somewhere else. However, when it comes to robotics, this communication introduces a lot of weight time or latency. So, it's usually preferable to have that compute integrated into your robot. For this series specifically, we'll be using the Recomputer from Seed Studios, which brings the Nvidia hardware together with all the different parapherics like cameras and different packages like the Jetack that allow you to very optimally leverage the GPUs. Whichever hardware you have, on top of it, you're going to be running an operating system. You're probably used to working with an operating system like Windows or Mac, but when it comes to robotics, everything runs on Linux. Most famously, Ross barely works on anything else, and CUDA is also a first class citizen. Specifically, you'd want to be running Ubuntu. Now, this works a little bit differently than working with UI first components like Windows. You'll be living in the terminal. This is a codef first interface that allows you to interact with everything that's happening on your computer. From navigating folders to editing files, everything will be happening from here. There's a few commands that you should be familiar with and get used to as we're going to be running a lot of things from the terminal. More details below. So, you have your computer and your operating system and you want to start coding. You'll need to learn a programming language. There's a lot of different ones out there, each with their own benefits from Java to C and C++. But because we're going to be running with a lot of AI and data science components, our language of choice is going to be Python. Now, luckily, this is a very accessible and easy to get started with language if you're just getting into programming, and it shares a lot of the core concepts with a lot of other languages. You'll need to be familiar with things like variables, lists, and dictionaries, functions, and even classes. And while more recently it's become more popular to use a lot of AI agents to help you with these tasks, it is very important that you can at least recognize and understand good code. So even if you don't plan to write a lot of the code yourself, you really need to understand the programming basics. So a good grasp of Python is essential. Not to say that you're going to be writing everything from scratch, there's a lot of libraries and utilities that help you when you're programming from the IDE that you work in which can help you with debugging and different utilities along the way. You can use something like VS Code or on this channel we've used a lot of PyCharm. You can organize your work in files or you can run in notebooks that allow you to have different code cells that are run on a kernel. This can speed up the process and make it a little bit easier. And we have things like Jupyter notebooks or Jupyter Labs that allow you to very quickly experiment and get things going. Even when you have a more robust setup, there's things like UV, which replaces the old pip and VM, allowing you to create environments, freeze packages, and get all the utilities you need in order, which then allows you to use Python for AI or data science or machine learning, deep learning, artificial intelligence. There's a lot of discussion about where all these topics and terms go. I've been talking about it for more than 5 years and I have a bachelor in data science which is the study that allows you to bring insights out of data and create things like models. If these models learn to get better from data over time, this is called machine learning. And if we're creating more advanced models like neural networks that mimic the way that the brain works with neuron firings and different functions, this is deep learning. And this is mostly what we'll be using for advanced robotics. Whereas data science and basic machine learning models used to only focus on numbers doing things like regressions and classifications, with deep learning, we can process images and videos, which allows the robot to process things from its sensors. Especially with the new developments with foundational models and vision, language, action models. These are very powerful tools that allow your robot to collect information from all the sensors, process it, and decide what to do based on that. Not to say that you need to be a statistics and data structure and algorithm expert, but it is good to have some of these concepts down. For example, tensors. These are multi-dimensional data structures that allow us to work with things like images and do complex computations on them. I have some videos on this as well, but to give a very quick explanation, you have things like scalers or numbers which are one-dimensionals. You have lists, you have matrices, and tensors take it one step further, which allow us to compute very complex data. which means that neural networks are just math on tensors. And that's enough for now. Again, it's good to have an understanding of this, but that's not to say that you're going to be doing matrix multiplication by hand. You have a lot of tools and libraries like PyTorch or TensorFlow that have already been optimized to work with these structures. You can import a lot of these libraries and use them directly. And this is where the open source community comes in as well. In most of the work we're doing, we're going to be using inference, which means running data against models that have already been trained and exist rather than building them from scratch. We can import these models from places like Psychitlearn or Hugging Face along with data sets that we can use for our robotics projects. So again, having a highle understanding and knowing what you need to use is most important part. And to bring it back to the rest of our working model, we have things like tensor RT that allows us to work with tensors in an optimized way for CUDA and for the GPU units that we'll be running our computations on. Now, by now things have gotten a little bit complicated and with that comes a lot of friction. For all these components to work together, they have to be the right version at the right time so that they're all compatible. We're talking the right Python version, all the different packages and libraries that you've imported along with the right CUDA version and the right Ross version with the right operating system. This can get a little bit frustrating for people who are new to it like I was literally a few weeks ago. But not to worry, we also have a few systems that are meant to help with this pain. Enter Docker. This is a way for us to freeze an entire environment in a state where everything works together and have it as a container. So in theory, you can have a container with Linux on it that runs all the other components smoothly together in harmony and just deploy it wherever is needed. Not to say that nothing will ever go wrong again, but this is a great tool to help you with this. And there's even more robotic specific tooling like this such as robot stack or pixie that help you ease this pain even more. Great. So we have our setup running smoothly. What now? We start giving our robot senses. A lot of the focus will be on vision, computer vision, which allows the robot to process images and videos and understand what's happening in them. Here's where libraries like OpenCV that have been reliably doing this for more than 25 years come in to save the day. There's a lot of functionalities here based on mathematical processes that allow us to do things like segmentation, classification, detection, even without rigging AI into it, all the way to using deep learning models like YOLO to do really advanced and real-time running stuff. If you want to do anything with cameras or images, it's very important to get comfortable with OpenCV libraries as well as the latest most popular models which at this moment is YOLO. And this combination should already get you quite far. And this covers the AIM ML data science specific part. Then we want this to be compatible with our actual robot and hardware. This is where Ross comes in. Love it or hate it, it's currently the most popular and well adopted operating system for robotics. It allows for different components to talk to each other and work together. A lot of the things you'll be seeing on the market come with Ross out of the box, which allow us to get up and running without having to write specific code for every single camera or rotor involved in your robot. This works with a system of nodes where all these components send messages on different topics to each other, allowing them to communicate and work harmoniously. These are the core concepts you need to get a good grasp on in order to work with Ross 2\. We're not using ROSS one anymore in 2026\. And this will allow you to work with a lot of different robots where people have already written the ROSS implementation for them. So you don't have to write everything from scratch or reinvent the wheel every single time. So next time you look at a robot doing a lot of really fancy stuff, you will know there's probably some GPUs in there controlled by CUDA running Linux on top with a lot of different programs, a lot of different ROSS nodes controlling the movement controlling the camera which is running a pipeline with OpenCV and a deep learning model like YOLO. There's probably some Python architecture running PyTorch or Tensor RT. And the whole setup is probably a Docker container that can just run, take the images, run through the pipelines, decide what to do, and move the robot. All of these layers will be smoothly working together and you can work on whichever of them you want. And that's only the beginning. Now that we know what we need to know, we can actually get this series started. In every one of the following episodes, we'll be exploring one of these components, doing a deep dive, having a hands-on tutorial on it, and getting you familiar with everything you need to know. There's a GitHub repo with all the resources in the description provided by Seed Studios to go along with this hardware and this series. In the next episode, I'll be unboxing my very own RIM computer and going through the setup. You can get the very same one if you want to run on the same hardware for the rest of the series, and you can follow along at home. Make sure to subscribe so we can go along in this journey together. And let me know in the comments which of these parts you're already familiar with, which ones you're most excited to learn, and if I left anything out. I'm Ulia. Welcome back to Engineering. and I'll see you in the next

These are two very, very different robot arms, although they're both obviously adorably pink. This can run on 10 bucks. This, however, needs to run on Linux. Then, of course, we've got a lot of different hardware options in between as well. Do you need to spend thousands of euros to get into robotics? Isn't that a little excessive and inaccessible and restrictive and unnecessary? Well, yes and no. You might not like it, but it really depends on what you actually want to do. So before we get back to our regularly scheduled program of going from zero all the way to physical AI and all the steps in between, let's take a minute and address the elephant in the room. Let's talk about costs. If we're talking about simple projects that have to do with electronics being controlled with a little bit of code, there's a lot of things you can do on a very limited budget. You want to build a robot arm, one of the most popular applications there is, you can do it with this Raspberry Pi Pico that literally costs 10 bucks. You may have to spend a little bit more on the servo motors and depending if you have a 3D printer or not, but you can build a full robot arm for under 50 bucks. However, I wouldn't really call this an application a physical AI if you want to build its big sister. However, this can run on 10 bucks. This however needs to run on Linux and it needs to run very big models. So, you may then be looking at one of these boards where the price tag does indeed start to go up. And of course, these are more or less extremes in this range. Although there are even cheaper boards out there than the 10 bucks Pico, and there's definitely more expensive boards out there than even this one. After all, something like the L robot, although quite advanced compared to the electronics projects makers might be used to, is actually one of the most affordable entry level into robotics. Then, of course, we've got a lot of different hardware options in between as well. What most people might be used to starting with is the Arduino Uno. This is about 40 to 60 bucks depending on where you get it from and it can get you quite far with a lot of variety of projects. However, this is only a microcontroller. If we want to go beyond the electronics projects and use it as an actual computer, we need a microprocessor. And those will be the Raspberry Pies, for example. This is the Raspberry Pi 3 that I've had around since I was in high school and the Raspberry Pi 5\. Now, these can range between 60 to almost 300 bucks because of the crisis that we have right now with CPUs. So, let's break it down tier by tier. The simplest application that you would be able to get away with is using microcontrollers like this Raspberry Pi Pico that can control a few servo motors or different sensors. However, you might need additional boards if you add in more components. If we want to go from five to 10 servo motors, we need a particular servo board that goes on top of it. So, the hardware costs also double. Similarly, when we're working with the Arduino, we can also add hats on top of this to create more complex applications. So if we go from this to this for example, we have an Arduino style board and a different hat on top that is able to control the different motors that control the wheels as well as the distance sensor and the camera and the little servo in here. All of these being put together to create the simplest, cheapest form of a self-driving car. This can follow a black line by looking at the signal and deciding if we want to go left or right. So, if you think of AI as a bunch of if statements in a trench code, then yes, we can have an AI self-driving car for under $100. However, if we want to do something more complex than if then statements, we're going to need more compute power. So, as I mentioned, the next step after a microcontroller is a microprocessor. We can use the Raspberry Pi basically as a smaller computer, so you're able to run more code and create different applications, and you can even run local models. However, the compute and the CPU on this is also limited. So, what you will see when we have more complex builds and projects and is that we'll also have a different driver on top of it. We've seen an example of this on the channel while we've built this robot dog. Besides running on the Raspberry Pi 5, it also has this top hat that has more functionalities in it. Being able to control a lot more server motors, the camera, the distance sensor, having speakers in it, and much more. So, we were able to run models to communicate and have the robot respond to us. However, there was quite a bit of lag and the responses were quite slow and the dog was only able to talk but not also control the movements at the same time. Then we've also seen more robotics applications like the Richi Mini which was also able to talk to us but also able to dance and perform more actions and we even managed to deploy a deep learning model on it. However, the Reichi Mini version that I had was the light version which had to be connected to your computer. So, the actual models run on your hardware rather than the Reichi Mini itself. The newest version of the Reichi Mini also has a Raspberry Pi in it. So, it's able to again run some models locally, but there will always be limitations and it's not really a GPU powered robot. So, if the projects that you have in mind are more like these, then absolutely microcontrollers and microprocessors and having a budget for a board for under $300 is more than enough. For both of these projects, we needed additional drivers or hats on top of the board to make it possible. So, don't forget that that cost will also add up. And if you want to do more advanced things like computer vision, there's also options to do that with hardware like this, with things like the AI camera for Raspberry Pi, which can also run things like detection and classification on its own hardware, and that's about 60 bucks as well. And I will continue to show those on this channel. However, if we are, like I said, wanting to go more towards the physical AI space, and I'm talking about the LE robot arm, which very similarly is 3D printed and has a few server motors controlling the movements. This arm can be teleoperated. We can record different episodes of the movement with different camera angles and analytics. And we can train different types of models to learn to imitate these movements and perform them autonomously. So you can have this arm pick up different things and put them in a different place or even perform household tasks like folding your laundry which to me is the whole purpose of robotics. I don't want them to dance. I want them to do chores and things that I don't want to do. we are going to have to look at the more expensive hardware as well. That being said, there's also a lot of tiers and levels when it comes to that. Even if we just take Nvidia, even if we just take Jetson, simply looking up Jetson board brings back an overwhelming amount of hardware options. And it can be a little bit confusing and intimidating. And if you see the really sparkly box, you have gone to the wrong place. You don't need to freak out. You can back away. We don't need to look at that yet. Let's start with the basics. The entry point to the Nvidia hardware is the Orin Nano. It used to just be the Nano, but that has been discontinued and is no longer being supported. However, it's been around for a very long time. So, most interesting projects or builds that you'll find out there might be referencing it, and that might be very confusing. I know it was for me. This is just one example of many, many other builds out there that have been around for a really long time and sadly are not an option anymore. Instead, the entry point now is the Jetson Orin Nano. And we're also starting to talk about measuring the performance of these different boards in something called TOPS. This means Terra operations per second. And it's a way to calculate how many things the board can do and also a simple way to compare them from one to the next. Although that is also quite a simplistic way to approach it and there's way more features that you should be taking into account. However, I am not a hardware girly. I come from data science and a lot of this stuff, I'll be honest, is a little bit confusing to me. There's a lot of YouTubers out there who explain it a lot better, but using something like TOPS is enough to give us a general idea of what you might be looking at. For example, like I said, if you want to run a simple VA model, we're looking at about 60 to 100 tops. So then you can look for hardware that covers that. So the entry level of the Jetson Orin Nano will be just a little below that allowing us to run inference but not fine-tune a model. We'd have to go one step further to the Orin NX series for that. And for most of the things that we'll be looking at in this series and on this channel for the foreseeable future, I'd say that's about the top of where we can stop for now. Of course, if you want to build industrial robots or things that run in factories or are a lot fancier, we will be going further and who knows, maybe next year we'll get to that. But for now, let's not make it too overwhelming. What it comes down to is will you want to build your own models? How many different cameras would you want to take in at the same time? How big are the models that you're going to run? Is going to be based on pictures or live video or having multimodal data coming in together. Things like that should help you decide between the Orin Nano or NX or if you even need to go a little bit above that, which may lead you to pick the module that you want to use. However, you'll also notice that for each module, there's also a lot of options depending on the carrier board that you want to attach it to. Very similar to what we explained with the microcontroller and the AI hat, they might go on top of it. The modules also need to live on top of another board that connected some more peripherics and can also do some of the computations. So, in this case, this little thing over here is the Jetson Orin. And this is a really cool top-of-the-line carrier board that does a lot of other functions around it. Depending again on what your project requires, you can swap that out for a different type of board. They can categorize those as classic, super, or robotics. Depending on the type of application you want to integrate it within. On the Seed Robotics website, there's actually a wizard that you can click through to kind of see the kind of applications you want to build and help you narrow down the selection or you can look at the official tables from Nvidia. Although, I will say there's a lot of numbers and specs out there. So, that can be a little bit intimidating as well. So, like I said, you can also just take the TOPS number and try to look up the types of model you want to work with and how much they require and trying to find a range that you're comfortable with, both in terms of what you want to build and in terms of budget. But to not make that too overwhelming, if this is your first time going beyond Raspberry Pi because you want to get into the world of physical AI, that's what this series is going to help with. looking at my audience and the things that we're trying to build together. If you come from meatronics or electronics or data science and you want to explore this physical AI space, something along this tier three solutions of the Jetson Orin Nano or NX Super or not is probably what you want to be looking at. While at the same time, you know, don't count the Raspberry Pi out of the race just yet. The 5 is actually quite performant as well. And with the Pi Hat, there are quite a lot of applications that we can run on that as well. and we can try to compare the performance as we go through things like the computer vision sections and the VA sections. I'll try to really show the difference and be very honest about which hardware works best for what. So, quick fire round to finish up. If you just want to make electronic projects, you can go quite far and do a lot of really fun stuff with a microcontroller board at somewhere between 10, 50, 60 bucks and just stick with that. you want to lean more into programming, running something like Linux and starting to experiment with ROSS, you probably need a microprocessor and work with one of the latest Raspberry Pies and using something like an AI top hat. If you want to work with the latest models or run more custom vision algorithms, if you want to enter the physical AI space and run with bigger models and more autonomous behavior, you're going to want to have something with a GPU and with a decent amount of tops or of course develop a lot of these on your personal computer or laptop if this happens to have some GPU in it as well. Hope this has given you some clarity about the space and what is available and what is needed. And the next episode will go ahead with our physical AI series. I'm going to walk you through the setup that you need to put everything together on a board like this. So, we're talking about installing Linux and Ross and the Jetson lab and SIM and everything we need to run with computer vision, different types of models, Docker and so on and so forth. So, it's going to be a very exciting journey ahead. I'm Ulia. Welcome back to engineering and I'll see you in the next
